questions.passage:
The philosopher Karl Popper identified what he called the paradox of tolerance: a society committed to tolerating all viewpoints contains the seeds of its own destruction, because unlimited tolerance extends protection to those who would, given the opportunity, abolish tolerance itself. Popper’s resolution was unambiguous — a tolerant society must reserve the right to suppress intolerant movements when they pose a genuine threat to the open order.

This formulation, however elegant, conceals a dangerous ambiguity. Who decides when intolerance has crossed from the merely offensive into the genuinely threatening? History offers no shortage of examples in which the vocabulary of protecting tolerance served as a pretext for silencing legitimate dissent. The McCarthyist purges, which framed political nonconformity as an existential threat to democratic values, illustrate how readily the exception can swallow the rule.

Contemporary legal theorists have attempted to navigate this tension by distinguishing between viewpoint-based restrictions — which suppress ideas themselves — and conduct-based restrictions, which target specific harmful actions regardless of the ideology motivating them. On this account, a society need not ban hateful beliefs to protect itself; it need only ensure that beliefs do not translate into coordinated harm. The advantage of this framework is its procedural neutrality: it avoids the epistemically perilous task of adjudicating which ideas are too dangerous to express, focusing instead on observable conduct.

Critics of the conduct-based approach counter that it arrives too late. By the time intolerant ideology has organized into actionable harm, the institutional damage may already be irreversible. They argue that ideas themselves possess causal force — that sustained public advocacy of exclusion erodes the social trust on which pluralistic institutions depend, even when no single act of speech constitutes a direct incitement. The question, then, is not whether ideas have consequences, but whether those consequences can be addressed without granting the state an authority that has historically proven impossible to contain.
questions.stem: The author’s central argument is best described as:

choices.label: A
choices.text: an endorsement of Popper’s original resolution as the most practical approach to protecting tolerance

choices.label: B
choices.text: a defense of the conduct-based approach as superior to both Popper’s formulation and viewpoint restrictions

choices.label: C
choices.text: an exploration of the unresolved tension between suppressing intolerant ideas and preventing state overreach

choices.label: D
choices.text: a rejection of all restrictions on speech as incompatible with genuine democratic tolerance

explanations.A: The author describes Popper’s resolution as ‘unambiguous’ but immediately identifies a ‘dangerous ambiguity’ within it and cites the McCarthyist purges as an example of its misuse. This critical engagement signals that the author views Popper’s position as incomplete or problematic, not as a practical solution to endorse.
explanations.B: While the author does present the conduct-based approach favorably (‘procedural neutrality,’ avoids ‘epistemically perilous’ judgments), the passage then gives substantive space to critics who argue it ‘arrives too late.’ The author does not resolve this debate in favor of the conduct-based approach — the passage ends with an open question, not a settled conclusion.
explanations.C: Correct. The passage moves through a dialectical structure: Popper’s resolution (thesis) → its vulnerability to abuse (complication) → the conduct-based alternative (partial resolution) → the critique that it is insufficient (counter-complication) → an open question about whether ideas can be addressed without dangerous state authority. The final sentence frames the core tension without resolving it, indicating the author’s purpose is to map the dilemma rather than advocate for one position. The passage is exploratory, not argumentative in a single direction.
explanations.D: The author never advocates for unlimited tolerance or unrestricted speech. The passage takes seriously the concern that intolerant ideas can cause real harm (‘sustained public advocacy of exclusion erodes social trust’). A blanket rejection of all restrictions is inconsistent with the author’s acknowledgment that ideas ‘possess causal force.’

questions.stem: The author most likely references the McCarthyist purges in order to:

choices.label: A
choices.text: provide historical evidence that intolerant movements always succeed when tolerated

choices.label: B
choices.text: illustrate how the principle of suppressing intolerance can be co-opted to silence legitimate dissent

choices.label: C
choices.text: argue that the United States has historically been too tolerant of political nonconformity

choices.label: D
choices.text: demonstrate that Popper’s paradox is a purely theoretical concern with no real-world relevance

explanations.A: The McCarthyist purges were not an example of intolerant movements succeeding through tolerance. They were an example of the state using the rhetoric of protecting democracy to suppress dissent. The reference supports the argument about the dangers of granting suppressive authority, not about the dangers of tolerating intolerance.
explanations.B: Correct. The McCarthyist purges are introduced immediately after the author identifies the ‘dangerous ambiguity’ in Popper’s resolution — the question of who decides when intolerance is threatening. McCarthy’s movement ‘framed political nonconformity as an existential threat to democratic values,’ using the language of protecting the open society to justify suppressing ideas that were merely unpopular, not genuinely threatening to democratic institutions. The example serves as concrete evidence for the author’s concern that ‘the exception can swallow the rule’ — that granting authority to suppress intolerance creates a tool that can be turned against legitimate political expression.
explanations.C: McCarthyism was characterized by excessive intolerance of political dissent, not excessive tolerance. The author presents this period as a cautionary tale about state overreach, not as evidence that more tolerance was needed. This reading inverts the author’s point.
explanations.D: The McCarthyist example demonstrates the opposite — that Popper’s paradox has very real practical consequences. The author uses the historical reference precisely to show that the tension Popper identified has played out in concrete political episodes, not to dismiss the paradox as purely theoretical.

questions.stem: Based on the passage, the ‘critics of the conduct-based approach’ would most likely agree with which of the following statements?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The state should have unrestricted authority to suppress any ideas it deems intolerant

choices.label: B
choices.text: Procedural neutrality is more important than preventing ideological harm to democratic institutions

choices.label: C
choices.text: Prohibiting harmful actions alone is insufficient because ideological movements can undermine institutions before reaching the threshold of actionable conduct

choices.label: D
choices.text: The McCarthyist purges were a justified response to genuine threats against democratic values

explanations.A: The passage does not attribute a call for unrestricted state authority to the critics. Their argument is that the conduct-based approach is too reactive, not that unlimited suppression is appropriate. The final sentence acknowledges the legitimate concern about state authority — even the critics implicitly accept that granting such authority is problematic. Their position is that the conduct-based approach has a specific flaw (arriving too late), not that any alternative is cost-free.
explanations.B: This is the position the critics are arguing against, not one they would endorse. The conduct-based approach values procedural neutrality (not judging which ideas are dangerous). The critics contend that this procedural neutrality comes at too high a cost — it permits ideological erosion to proceed unchecked until it is too late.
explanations.C: Correct. The critics argue that ‘by the time intolerant ideology has organized into actionable harm, the institutional damage may already be irreversible.’ Their core claim is that ideas have causal force before they crystallize into specific criminal conduct — ‘sustained public advocacy of exclusion erodes the social trust on which pluralistic institutions depend, even when no single act of speech constitutes a direct incitement.’ They view the conduct threshold as too high, allowing cumulative harm to accumulate below the line of actionability.
explanations.D: Nothing in the passage attributes this view to the critics. The author presents McCarthyism as an example of state overreach, and there is no indication that the critics of the conduct-based approach would view McCarthyism favorably. Endorsing McCarthy’s methods would undercut their own credibility, since McCarthyism exemplifies the very state authority whose risks they implicitly acknowledge.

questions.stem: Which of the following, if true, would most strengthen the conduct-based approach as described in the passage?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A longitudinal study showing that societies with strong hate-speech laws have lower rates of political violence than societies relying on conduct-based restrictions

choices.label: B
choices.text: Evidence that early intervention against hateful speech is more effective than prosecution of completed hate crimes

choices.label: C
choices.text: Historical analysis demonstrating that societies using conduct-based restrictions maintained democratic institutions as effectively as those using viewpoint-based restrictions, without the associated risks of state censorship

choices.label: D
choices.text: A philosophical argument that all ideas, regardless of content, deserve equal legal protection

explanations.A: This would weaken the conduct-based approach by suggesting that viewpoint-based restrictions (hate-speech laws) produce better outcomes. If restricting ideas directly reduces political violence more effectively, the procedural neutrality of the conduct-based approach comes at a real cost in terms of safety.
explanations.B: This supports the critics’ position, not the conduct-based approach. If early intervention (before conduct reaches an actionable threshold) is more effective, then waiting for harmful conduct — which is what the conduct-based approach requires — is suboptimal. This evidence would strengthen the case for addressing ideas before they organize into action.
explanations.C: Correct. The conduct-based approach’s central claim is that societies can protect themselves by targeting harmful actions without needing to judge which ideas are dangerous — and that this avoids the historically demonstrated risks of state censorship. If historical evidence showed that this approach achieved equivalent institutional protection without the collateral damage of viewpoint restrictions, both of its claimed advantages would be empirically supported: equal effectiveness plus reduced risk of state overreach.
explanations.D: While this is philosophically consistent with the conduct-based approach’s neutrality toward ideas, a purely philosophical argument does not address the practical concern raised by critics — that ideas cause cumulative institutional damage before conduct thresholds are reached. The conduct-based approach needs empirical support showing that its framework works in practice, not just philosophical justification for its principles.

questions.stem: The author’s phrase ‘the exception can swallow the rule’ most nearly means that:

choices.label: A
choices.text: exceptional individuals can overcome societal rules through personal courage

choices.label: B
choices.text: a provision intended as a limited exception to a principle can expand until the principle itself is effectively nullified

choices.label: C
choices.text: legal exceptions to free speech are always unconstitutional

choices.label: D
choices.text: intolerant ideas will inevitably overwhelm tolerant societies regardless of any protective measures

explanations.A: This interprets ‘exception’ as referring to exceptional people, but in context the phrase refers to a logical exception carved into a general principle. The author is discussing how a narrow exception to tolerance (suppressing intolerance) can expand to consume tolerance itself, not how individuals overcome rules.
explanations.B: Correct. The phrase appears in the context of the McCarthyist example, where ‘the vocabulary of protecting tolerance served as a pretext for silencing legitimate dissent.’ The ‘rule’ is the general principle of tolerance. The ‘exception’ is the Popperian provision that intolerance may be suppressed when threatening. The metaphor warns that this exception — intended to be narrow and protective — can expand through political manipulation until the exception (suppression) becomes the norm and the rule (tolerance) becomes effectively meaningless. It is a warning about the slippery slope from principled exception to systematic abuse.
explanations.C: The passage does not make constitutional claims. The author is engaged in philosophical and political analysis, not legal interpretation. Furthermore, the author does not argue that all exceptions are illegitimate — only that they carry inherent risks of expansion.
explanations.D: The phrase does not express fatalism about the inevitability of intolerant triumph. Rather, it warns about a specific institutional risk: that the mechanism designed to protect tolerance can itself become a tool of intolerance. This is a contingent risk (it can happen, as McCarthyism shows), not an inevitable outcome.

questions.passage:
The distinction between therapy and enhancement has long served as the moral compass of medical practice. Therapy restores a patient to normal functioning; enhancement pushes beyond the normal into the aspirational. A physician who prescribes insulin to a diabetic is engaged in therapy; one who prescribes growth hormone to a child of average height whose parents desire a taller adult is, on most accounts, engaged in enhancement. The intuitive moral difference seems clear: therapy responds to suffering, while enhancement serves preference.

Yet this distinction dissolves under scrutiny. The concept of ‘normal functioning’ on which it depends is not a biological given but a statistical and social construction. The boundary between the fifth percentile of a trait distribution — where we locate pathology — and the twentieth is not inscribed in nature but drawn by committees. Moreover, the suffering caused by falling below a socially constructed norm can be as genuine as the suffering caused by disease. The short-statured child who is relentlessly bullied experiences real harm; the claim that this harm does not warrant medical intervention because its source is social rather than biological imposes an arbitrary hierarchy of suffering.

Philosophers of a libertarian persuasion argue that the therapy-enhancement distinction should be abandoned altogether. If an intervention is safe, and the individual (or guardian) provides informed consent, then the moral evaluation should concern itself with autonomy and risk, not with the metaphysically dubious question of whether the intervention restores or augments. The purpose of medicine, on this view, is to serve human wellbeing — and wellbeing does not respect the boundary between the statistically normal and the statistically exceptional.

Opponents worry that abandoning the distinction opens medicine to the logic of the market, transforming physicians from healers into service providers and patients into consumers. More troublingly, in a society of unequal resources, enhancement technologies would flow disproportionately to those who can afford them, converting biological equality into yet another arena of socioeconomic stratification. The therapy-enhancement distinction, however philosophically imperfect, may serve as a necessary brake on a trajectory toward a world in which the advantages of wealth extend to the genome itself.
questions.stem: The author presents the libertarian argument for abandoning the therapy-enhancement distinction primarily as:

choices.label: A
choices.text: the position the author personally endorses as morally superior

choices.label: B
choices.text: a logically coherent position whose practical consequences the author views with concern

choices.label: C
choices.text: a fringe philosophical view with no mainstream support

choices.label: D
choices.text: a straw man constructed to make the opponents’ position appear stronger

explanations.A: While the author gives the libertarian position a fair and substantive presentation, the final paragraph’s concerns about market logic, socioeconomic stratification, and advantages extending ‘to the genome itself’ indicate discomfort with the position’s implications. The author does not endorse the libertarian view but rather presents it as one pole of a genuine dilemma.
explanations.B: Correct. The author presents the libertarian argument with intellectual respect: it correctly identifies the philosophical weakness of the therapy-enhancement distinction (the socially constructed nature of ‘normal’), and its proposed alternative (focus on autonomy and risk) is presented as internally coherent. However, the passage then pivots to concerns about practical consequences — commodification of medicine, inequality of access, and genomic stratification. The structure suggests the author finds the argument philosophically persuasive but socially dangerous, viewing it as logically sound reasoning that leads to troubling outcomes.
explanations.C: The author does not marginalize the libertarian position. It is presented as a serious philosophical argument with identifiable premises and conclusions, not as a fringe or eccentric view. The label ‘philosophers of a libertarian persuasion’ treats the position as an established school of thought, not an outlier.
explanations.D: A straw man is a deliberately weakened version of an opposing position. The libertarian argument is presented at its strongest — it identifies genuine philosophical problems with the therapy-enhancement distinction and offers a principled alternative based on autonomy and wellbeing. The author then critiques the consequences, not the reasoning, suggesting the engagement is genuine rather than strategic misrepresentation.

questions.stem: According to the passage, the example of a short-statured child who is bullied is used to argue that:

choices.label: A
choices.text: bullying is a medical condition that requires therapeutic intervention

choices.label: B
choices.text: growth hormone should be prescribed to all children below average height

choices.label: C
choices.text: the moral distinction between therapy and enhancement is undermined when socially caused suffering is as real as biologically caused suffering

choices.label: D
choices.text: social problems should always be addressed through medical rather than social interventions

explanations.A: The passage does not classify bullying as a medical condition. The point is about the source of suffering, not the classification of bullying. The example challenges the assumption that only biologically caused suffering warrants medical intervention, without claiming that the social cause (bullying) should itself be medicalized.
explanations.B: The passage does not make a specific policy recommendation about growth hormone prescription. The example is used philosophically — to interrogate the logical foundation of the therapy-enhancement distinction — not to advocate for a specific clinical practice. The author is questioning the conceptual boundary, not prescribing treatment protocols.
explanations.C: Correct. The example challenges the ‘arbitrary hierarchy of suffering’ that privileges biologically caused distress over socially caused distress. The therapy-enhancement distinction assumes that restoring ‘normal functioning’ addresses genuine suffering (therapy) while improving beyond normal addresses mere preference (enhancement). The bullied child’s example undermines this by showing that the harm caused by being below a socially defined norm can be just as real and severe as harm caused by disease. If the suffering is equivalent, why should the response be categorized differently based on whether the cause is biological or social? This erodes the moral intuition that therapy and enhancement are fundamentally different ethical categories.
explanations.D: The passage does not argue that social problems should be medicalized. The example is narrower: it questions whether the therapy-enhancement distinction can coherently exclude socially caused suffering from the domain of legitimate medical intervention. This is a challenge to a specific conceptual boundary, not a general endorsement of medical solutions to social problems.

questions.stem: The opponents’ argument that enhancement technologies would ‘convert biological equality into yet another arena of socioeconomic stratification’ is most analogous to which of the following concerns?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Concern that access to quality public education varies by neighborhood wealth, creating unequal opportunities from birth

choices.label: B
choices.text: Concern that universal healthcare systems reduce the quality of care by eliminating market competition

choices.label: C
choices.text: Concern that genetic testing for inherited diseases violates patient privacy rights

choices.label: D
choices.text: Concern that pharmaceutical companies charge too much for essential medications

explanations.A: Correct. The opponents worry that enhancement technologies, available primarily to the wealthy, would create biological advantages that compound existing socioeconomic advantages — wealth would translate into better genes, faster cognition, or stronger bodies, making inequality self-reinforcing across generations. This is structurally analogous to the concern about education: when access to quality schooling depends on family wealth (through property taxes, neighborhood zoning, private school tuition), an institution that should equalize opportunity instead amplifies pre-existing inequality. Both concerns involve a domain that could serve as an equalizer (education/biological endowment) being captured by economic stratification, creating cascading advantages for those already privileged.
explanations.B: This concern is about the quality effects of removing market mechanisms, not about unequal access creating stratification. The opponents’ argument is specifically about how unequal access to enhancement converts existing inequality into new domains, not about the effects of eliminating competition on quality.
explanations.C: Privacy concerns about genetic testing involve informational rights and autonomy, not the socioeconomic stratification that is central to the opponents’ argument. The opponents are concerned about distributive justice — who gets access to enhancement — not about the privacy implications of genetic information.
explanations.D: While high medication costs are an access issue, the opponents’ concern is more structural. They are not simply arguing that enhancements will be expensive (a price problem), but that enhancement will create a new axis of biological inequality that mirrors and reinforces socioeconomic inequality (a justice problem). The concern is about the systemic consequences of unequal access, not just the affordability of individual products.

questions.stem: The author describes the therapy-enhancement distinction as ‘however philosophically imperfect’ yet potentially a ‘necessary brake.’ This characterization most strongly implies that the author believes:

choices.label: A
choices.text: philosophical perfection should always take precedence over practical utility

choices.label: B
choices.text: a flawed conceptual framework can still serve an important regulatory function

choices.label: C
choices.text: the distinction is philosophically sound despite its critics

choices.label: D
choices.text: enhancement technologies should be permanently banned to prevent inequality

explanations.A: The author’s language implies the opposite priority. By acknowledging the distinction’s philosophical imperfections while defending its practical utility as a ‘brake,’ the author suggests that practical consequences can outweigh conceptual elegance. If philosophical perfection were the overriding concern, the author would side with the libertarians, whose position is presented as the more logically coherent one.
explanations.B: Correct. The phrase ‘however philosophically imperfect’ concedes the libertarian critique — the therapy-enhancement distinction does rest on a socially constructed and somewhat arbitrary notion of normal functioning. Yet ‘necessary brake’ asserts a pragmatic defense: even a conceptually flawed distinction can serve as a valuable restraint against socially harmful trajectories. The author is making a consequentialist argument for maintaining an imperfect rule: its benefits (preventing genomic stratification, preserving medicine’s healing identity) outweigh its conceptual weaknesses.
explanations.C: The author explicitly concedes that the distinction is philosophically imperfect, contradicting the claim that it is philosophically sound. The second paragraph systematically undermines its conceptual foundations. The defense offered in the final paragraph is practical, not philosophical.
explanations.D: The passage does not advocate for a permanent ban on enhancement technologies. The distinction serves as a ‘brake’ — a restraining mechanism — not an absolute prohibition. A brake slows or limits; it does not forbid entirely. The author is arguing for caution and regulatory restraint, not for categorical prohibition.

questions.stem: Which of the following claims, if advanced by the author, would be most inconsistent with the passage’s overall argument?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The concept of ‘normal functioning’ will continue to evolve as medical technology advances

choices.label: B
choices.text: Societies should carefully regulate enhancement technologies to prevent exacerbation of inequality

choices.label: C
choices.text: The therapy-enhancement distinction provides a philosophically rigorous foundation for medical ethics that should never be questioned

choices.label: D
choices.text: Libertarian arguments about autonomy deserve serious consideration even when their conclusions are concerning

explanations.A: This is entirely consistent with the passage. The author argues that ‘normal functioning’ is a social construction drawn by committees, not a biological given. Acknowledging that this construction evolves with technology is a natural extension of the passage’s analysis.
explanations.B: This aligns with the passage’s concluding concern about socioeconomic stratification and the characterization of the therapy-enhancement distinction as a ‘necessary brake.’ Advocating for regulation to prevent inequality is fully consistent with the opponents’ position, which the author treats sympathetically.
explanations.C: Correct. This directly contradicts the passage’s argument. The author spends the second paragraph systematically demonstrating that the therapy-enhancement distinction is philosophically imperfect — that ‘normal functioning’ is socially constructed, the boundary between pathology and normalcy is arbitrary, and the distinction imposes an ‘arbitrary hierarchy of suffering.’ Calling the distinction ‘philosophically rigorous’ and saying it ‘should never be questioned’ would contradict the author’s own critical analysis.
explanations.D: This is consistent with the passage’s treatment of the libertarian position. The author presents the argument substantively and identifies its logical strengths before raising concerns about consequences. This approach — taking an argument seriously while questioning its implications — is exactly what the passage models.

questions.passage:
The ancient Stoics held that the good life consists in the exercise of virtue in accordance with nature, and that external circumstances — wealth, health, reputation — are morally indifferent. A person enslaved, impoverished, or dying of disease could be as fully virtuous, and therefore as happy, as the wealthiest emperor. Happiness, on this account, is entirely internal: it consists in the rational governance of one’s desires and judgments, a citadel of the mind that no external force can breach.

This doctrine has resurfaced in contemporary psychology under the banner of resilience research and cognitive-behavioral therapy, which emphasize that subjective wellbeing depends more on how individuals interpret events than on the events themselves. The therapeutic injunction to “reframe” adversity — to find meaning in suffering, to adjust expectations, to cultivate gratitude regardless of circumstances — carries unmistakable Stoic resonances.

Yet there is a troubling dimension to this inheritance. When the Stoic framework migrates from personal philosophy to institutional prescription, it risks becoming an instrument of political quietism. If happiness is entirely a matter of attitude, then unjust social arrangements need not be changed — only the attitudes of those who suffer under them. The enslaved person’s capacity for virtue becomes, perversely, an argument against abolition: why restructure society when individuals can restructure their minds?

No serious Stoic philosopher made this argument explicitly. Marcus Aurelius, writing from the seat of imperial power, advocated justice alongside inner discipline. But the logic of radical internalism exerts a gravitational pull in this direction, and contemporary wellness culture — with its emphasis on individual mindset, gratitude journals, and meditation apps — frequently completes the trajectory the ancient Stoics left unfinished. The question is whether a philosophy of inner freedom can be maintained without inadvertently legitimizing the external conditions from which that inner freedom was meant to provide refuge.
questions.stem: The author’s attitude toward the Stoic philosophy of happiness is best characterized as:

choices.label: A
choices.text: entirely dismissive, viewing it as a tool of political oppression

choices.label: B
choices.text: uncritically admiring, endorsing its application in modern therapy

choices.label: C
choices.text: appreciative of its personal value while concerned about its political implications when institutionalized

choices.label: D
choices.text: neutral and detached, presenting arguments without personal evaluation

explanations.A: The author does not dismiss Stoicism entirely. The passage acknowledges its genuine philosophical content (the exercise of virtue, rational self-governance) and notes that ‘no serious Stoic philosopher’ made the politically quietist argument explicitly. The concern is about what happens when the philosophy migrates to institutional contexts, not about the philosophy’s inherent merit as a personal practice.
explanations.B: The author raises explicit concerns about the application of Stoic principles in contemporary wellness culture (‘gratitude journals and meditation apps’) and cognitive-behavioral therapy. The word ‘troubling’ in the third paragraph signals critical engagement, and the passage identifies a structural risk in the therapeutic application. This is not uncritical admiration.
explanations.C: Correct. The passage treats Stoicism with intellectual respect as a personal philosophy — the ‘citadel of the mind’ metaphor is presented without irony, and Marcus Aurelius is cited as someone who held inner discipline and justice together. However, the author identifies a structural risk: when radical internalism becomes institutional policy (‘institutional prescription’), it can function as ‘political quietism’ — an argument against changing unjust conditions. The concern is not with Stoicism as personal practice but with its instrumentalization as a substitute for social reform.
explanations.D: The author is not neutral. Words like ‘troubling,’ ‘perversely,’ and ‘gravitational pull’ reveal evaluative engagement. The final question is framed with evident concern about whether inner freedom might ‘inadvertently legitimize’ unjust conditions. While the author is balanced, they are not detached.

questions.stem: The author suggests that contemporary wellness culture ‘completes the trajectory the ancient Stoics left unfinished.’ In context, this phrase implies that:

choices.label: A
choices.text: modern wellness practices have improved upon Stoic philosophy by adding scientific rigor

choices.label: B
choices.text: contemporary wellness culture has carried the Stoic emphasis on individual mindset to its logical but problematic political conclusion

choices.label: C
choices.text: the Stoics would have endorsed meditation apps and gratitude journals had they been available

choices.label: D
choices.text: ancient Stoic philosophy was incomplete because it lacked practical tools for emotional regulation

explanations.A: The phrase is not a compliment. ‘Completing the trajectory’ refers to extending a logic to a conclusion the original thinkers did not reach — and the context makes clear this conclusion is problematic. The passage does not praise wellness culture for adding rigor but criticizes it for realizing a latent political implication.
explanations.B: Correct. The ‘unfinished trajectory’ is the move from ‘happiness is internal’ (philosophical insight) to ‘therefore external conditions need not change’ (political quietism). The ancient Stoics — especially Marcus Aurelius — maintained a commitment to justice alongside inner discipline, stopping short of the politically quietist conclusion. Contemporary wellness culture, with its exclusive emphasis on individual mindset, gratitude, and meditation, drops the justice component and implicitly accepts (or ignores) the social structures within which individuals practice their resilience. It ‘completes’ the trajectory by arriving at the conclusion the Stoics resisted: that adjusting attitudes is sufficient, and social change is unnecessary.
explanations.C: The author is not speculating about what the Stoics would have used. The phrase addresses the logical structure of ideas, not the historical preferences of ancient philosophers. Whether the Stoics would have liked meditation apps is irrelevant to the argument about political implications.
explanations.D: The ‘unfinished’ trajectory is not about practical tools but about political implications. The passage does not suggest that Stoicism lacked emotional regulation techniques — the Stoics had sophisticated practices of self-examination and cognitive reappraisal. What they left ‘unfinished’ was the political conclusion that radical internalism implicitly supports.

questions.stem: A public health official argues that resilience training programs should be implemented in low-income communities to help residents cope with chronic stress from poverty and environmental hazards. Based on the passage, the author would most likely respond by:

choices.label: A
choices.text: fully supporting the program as a practical application of proven Stoic principles

choices.label: B
choices.text: rejecting the program entirely because resilience training has no psychological benefits

choices.label: C
choices.text: questioning whether the program might function as a substitute for addressing the structural causes of poverty and environmental hazards

choices.label: D
choices.text: arguing that only ancient Stoic texts, not modern adaptations, should be used in therapeutic settings

explanations.A: The passage’s central concern is precisely about the institutional application of resilience-focused philosophy to populations living under unjust conditions. Full, uncritical support for the program would contradict the author’s argument that such applications risk becoming ‘instruments of political quietism’ that implicitly accept the structural conditions causing the suffering.
explanations.B: The author does not deny the psychological benefits of resilience training or cognitive reframing. The passage acknowledges that these approaches have ‘Stoic resonances’ and that subjective wellbeing does depend partly on interpretation. The concern is not that the approach is psychologically worthless but that it may be politically problematic when used as the primary response to systemic injustice.
explanations.C: Correct. This response directly applies the passage’s central argument. The author’s concern is that when radical internalism becomes institutional policy, it can substitute for structural change: ‘If happiness is entirely a matter of attitude, then unjust social arrangements need not be changed.’ A resilience training program in a community suffering from poverty and environmental hazards is precisely the scenario the author finds ‘troubling’ — it addresses the attitudes of those who suffer rather than the conditions that produce the suffering. The author would likely ask whether the program is being offered alongside efforts to address poverty and hazards, or instead of them.
explanations.D: The passage does not express preference for ancient texts over modern adaptations. The critique applies to the logical structure of radical internalism regardless of its historical packaging. Whether the resilience training uses Marcus Aurelius or a smartphone app is irrelevant to the political concern the author raises.

questions.stem: The author’s reference to Marcus Aurelius primarily serves to:

choices.label: A
choices.text: demonstrate that Stoic philosophy is only valuable when practiced by those in positions of power

choices.label: B
choices.text: concede that the Stoic tradition itself maintained a commitment to justice that complicates a purely quietist reading

choices.label: C
choices.text: argue that political leaders should adopt Stoic philosophy as their governing framework

choices.label: D
choices.text: provide evidence that the Stoics explicitly endorsed using inner freedom to justify social inequality

explanations.A: The reference to Marcus Aurelius’s imperial position is context, not a claim about who should practice Stoicism. The author is showing that even a Stoic with supreme political power maintained a commitment to justice — not that the philosophy is only for the powerful.
explanations.B: Correct. Marcus Aurelius is introduced immediately after the author raises the ‘troubling’ possibility that Stoicism could serve as political quietism. By noting that Aurelius ‘advocated justice alongside inner discipline,’ the author concedes that the Stoic tradition was not monolithically quietist — its greatest practitioner-philosopher held justice and inner freedom together. This complicates a simple reading of Stoicism as inherently conservative. The concession strengthens the author’s argument by showing intellectual honesty: the problem is not with Stoicism as the Stoics practiced it, but with what happens when its internalist logic is extracted and institutionalized without the accompanying commitment to justice.
explanations.C: The passage does not advocate for any political adoption of Stoic governance. Marcus Aurelius is cited as a historical example of balanced Stoicism, not as a model for contemporary political leadership.
explanations.D: The author explicitly states the opposite: ‘No serious Stoic philosopher made this argument explicitly.’ Marcus Aurelius is cited as evidence that the Stoics resisted the quietist conclusion, not that they endorsed it.

questions.stem: Which of the following best captures the passage’s final question about ‘a philosophy of inner freedom’?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Can individuals achieve inner peace without withdrawing entirely from political engagement?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Can the value of psychological resilience be preserved without implicitly accepting that unjust external conditions are acceptable?

choices.label: C
choices.text: Should philosophy focus exclusively on internal experience rather than external reality?

choices.label: D
choices.text: Is the Stoic concept of virtue compatible with modern democratic values?

explanations.A: This captures part of the tension but misses the specific concern. The passage is not about whether individuals should withdraw from politics but about whether the institutional promotion of inner-focused approaches inadvertently normalizes unjust conditions. The problem is systemic, not about individual political participation.
explanations.B: Correct. The final sentence asks whether inner freedom can be ‘maintained without inadvertently legitimizing the external conditions from which that inner freedom was meant to provide refuge.’ The core tension is between two values: (1) the genuine psychological benefit of cultivating inner resilience and reframing adversity, and (2) the risk that emphasizing inner adjustment implicitly signals that external conditions are fixed, natural, or acceptable. The passage asks whether these two aspects can be separated — whether one can advocate for inner freedom as a complement to, rather than substitute for, structural change.
explanations.C: This oversimplifies the passage’s question into a binary (internal vs. external) that the author explicitly rejects. The passage’s sophistication lies in arguing that both dimensions matter: inner freedom has genuine value, but it must not become a justification for external injustice. The question is about their interaction, not about choosing between them.
explanations.D: While an interesting question, it is not the one the passage poses. The final question is specifically about the relationship between internal philosophical practice and external political conditions — not about the compatibility of Stoic virtue with democratic theory.

questions.topic: Main Point of a Concluding Question
